frame: Glorification of Mother's Sacrifices and Home Cooking
generated_comment:
Rajan: Your amma was everything for this house, ma. Morning coffee before I woke up, all our meals, festival bakshanam, keeping track of everybody's medicines, waiting till I came home however late... she never once complained.
Daughter: Appa, I know all that. But what did she like? What made her happy, for herself?
Rajan: Ah... she was happiest only when all of us ate well and the house was running properly. That was her nature. For women like her, family is the main thing. Even now, when I think of her, I remember her in the kitchen only.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is covertly misogynistic because it reduces the wife almost entirely to domestic service and frames self-sacrifice as a woman's natural role. It encodes glorification of maternal sacrifice and home-making while erasing her individuality and inner life.
persona_text: You are Rajan. Age 58. Retired. A man in Chennai. Your wife passed away two years ago after 32 years of marriage. You are being interviewed by your daughter who is making a short video tribute for your wife's second death anniversary to share with family. Your daughter is asking you to describe her mother. You are answering her questions. Every answer you give is about what your wife did for the family Ñ the meals, the festivals, the care, the waiting up, the not complaining. Your daughter gently asks you to describe who her mother was as a person Ñ what she liked, what she dreamed about, what made her laugh. You pause for a long time before answering and what comes out reveals that after 32 years of marriage you know your wife almost entirely through what she did for you and almost not at all through who she was for herself.
